title: Sex gap in life expectancy at age 45
subtitle: The sex gap [period life expectancy](#dod:period-life-expectancy) (females minus males), for people who have reached the age of 45. Positive values indicate higher life expectancy among females; negative values indicate higher life expectancy among males.
caption:
Sex gap in life expectancy at age 45

The sex gap period life expectancy (females minus males), for people who have reached the age of 45. Positive values indicate higher life expectancy among females; negative values indicate higher life expectancy among males.

This line chart tracks the sex gap in life expectancy at age 45 for France, the United States, the United Kingdom, and Sweden from the mid-18th century through 2023. The vertical axis measures the gap in years, ranging from 0 to 7, where positive values denote that females have a higher life expectancy than males. The data for Sweden, represented by a blue line, spans the longest period, starting around 1751. For much of the 18th and 19th centuries, the Swedish gap fluctuates primarily between 1 and 3 years. The lines for France, the United States, and the United Kingdom begin later, between 1816 and 1841. Across all four nations, the gap widens significantly during the 20th century. France, shown in orange, reaches the highest peak on the chart, approaching 7 years around 1980 before declining to approximately 5 years by 2023. The United States and United Kingdom follow a similar trajectory, peaking above 6 years and just under 5.5 years respectively in the mid-to-late 20th century, before falling to roughly 4 years and 3.5 years. Sweden’s gap also rises in the 20th century, peaking near 5.5 years around 1980, then dropping to sit near 3 years in the most recent data points. By 2023, all four countries show a reduced gap compared to their late-20th-century highs, with values clustering between 3 and 5 years.
